question_text: A student observes smoke particles moving randomly under a microscope. Which statement best explains why the smoke particles move in this jerky, random way?

options.label: A
options.text: The smoke particles are being hit by invisible air molecules moving in random directions

options.label: B
options.text: The smoke particles are repelled by the glass slide beneath them

options.label: C
options.text: The microscope lens is vibrating and causing the particles to move

options.label: D
options.text: The smoke particles are evaporating and leaving the slide

correct_answer: A) The smoke particles are being hit by invisible air molecules moving in random directions
explanation: This is Brownian motion. The visible smoke particles are constantly bombarded by fast-moving, invisible air molecules from all directions, which causes them to move in a random zigzag path. This proves that particles in gases are in constant motion.
